aligned_pages.ppt_text:
Training Error and Generalization Error
• Training error (bias): model error on the training data
• Generalization error (variance): model error on new data
• Example: practice a future exam with past exams
• Doing well on past exams (training error) doesn’t guarantee a  good score on the future exam (generalization error)
• Student A gets 0 error on past exams by rote learning
• Student B  understands the reasons for givenanswers

bullets.ai_comment: The teacher introduces this topic by motivating it with a concrete observation: a 6-layer neural network may actually perform worse than a simpler 2-layer model, which seems counterintuitive given that deeper networks are supposed to be more powerful. This paradox sets the stage for the entire discussion — there are practical pitfalls in training deep networks that theory alone does not warn you about. The rest of the lecture is dedicated to unpacking several such practical aspects to help students train models more effectively in real-world scenarios.

generated_pages.ppt_text: Predict Who Will Repay TheirLoans •A lender hires you to investigate who will repay their  loans •You are given complete files on 100 •5 defaulted within 3 years Image creditdebt.org

aligned_segments.text: That is, I may want to predict who will repay their loan and who will default.
So in such a scenario, a lender helps you to investigate.
This problem.
And you are given the files of one hundred applicants.
Among these five, one hundred applicants, there are five of them defaulted within three years.

bullets.ai_comment: The core task introduced here is a binary prediction problem: determining which loan applicants will repay and which will default. The teacher frames this as a real-world scenario where a lender brings in a data analyst or data scientist to investigate. This type of problem is highly relevant in the finance and banking industry, where accurate predictions directly impact lending decisions and risk management.

bullets.ppt_text: A lender hires you to investigate who will repay their  loans
bullets.ai_comment: The teacher sets up the problem from the perspective of being hired by a lender, emphasizing the applied, professional context of the task. This framing highlights that the analyst's role is to use available data to solve a business problem — predicting loan repayment behavior. It underscores the practical importance of predictive modeling in financial decision-making.

bullets.ppt_text: You are given complete files on 100
bullets.ai_comment: The teacher specifies that the dataset consists of complete files on 100 loan applicants, meaning full background or financial information is available for each individual. This is the training dataset from which patterns will be extracted to build a predictive model. Having complete files is significant because missing data can complicate model training and reduce accuracy.

Out of the 100 applicants, the teacher clarifies that only 5 of them defaulted within a three-year period, meaning the remaining 95 successfully repaid their loans. This reveals a class imbalance in the dataset — a common and important challenge in machine learning where one outcome (default) is much rarer than the other (repayment). Recognizing this imbalance is critical because standard models may become biased toward predicting the majority class, requiring special handling techniques.

A Surprising Finding
• All 5 people who defaulted wore blue shirts during interviews
• Your model may find this strong signal as well

bullets.ai_comment: The teacher introduces a counterintuitive scenario to illustrate a fundamental machine learning pitfall. The setup involves building a credit default prediction model, where the data reveals an unexpected pattern. This example is designed to challenge students to think critically about whether a statistically observed pattern is actually meaningful or valid.

bullets.ppt_text: • All 5 people who defaulted wore blue shirts during interviews
bullets.ai_comment: The teacher describes a scenario where photos from loan interviews reveal that every single one of the five defaulters happened to be wearing a blue shirt. This is a deliberately absurd example to highlight how a model can latch onto a spurious correlation — a pattern that exists in the data purely by coincidence. There are two reasons this is not a valid signal: first, it's a classic case of overfitting on a tiny sample (only 5 people), and second, human logic tells us that shirt color has absolutely no causal relationship with financial default behavior.

bullets.ppt_text: • Your model may find this strong signal as well
bullets.ai_comment: The teacher warns that a machine learning model, lacking common sense, will treat the blue shirt correlation as a genuinely strong predictive feature because it perfectly separates defaulters from non-defaulters in the training data. This is the danger of overfitting — the model memorizes noise or coincidences in the training set rather than learning true underlying patterns. The practical takeaway is that feature validity must be evaluated not just statistically, but also through domain knowledge and human reasoning, to avoid building models that perform well on training data but fail completely in the real world.

Training Error and Generalization Error
• Training error (bias): model error on the training data
• Generalization error (variance): model error on new data
• Example: practice a future exam with past exams
• Doing well on past exams (training error) doesn’t guarantee a  good score on the future exam (generalization error)
• Student A gets 0 error on past exams by rote learning
• Student B  understands the reasons for givenanswers

aligned_segments.text: And this introduce our classic discussion of training error and generalization error.
Training error means, the error on the training data set and generalization error refers to your error on a new data.
And we always say that the performance on the training data does not represents the performance on a new data set.
This is very similar to we make practices for a.

bullets.ai_comment: This slide introduces one of the most fundamental concepts in machine learning: the distinction between how well a model performs on data it has already seen versus data it has never seen. The teacher frames this as a 'classic discussion,' signaling its central importance in the field. Understanding this distinction is the foundation for diagnosing underfitting, overfitting, and building models that truly generalize.

bullets.ppt_text: • Training error (bias): model error on the training data
bullets.ai_comment: Training error is the mistake the model makes on the exact data it was trained on. The teacher explains it simply as 'the error on the training data set.' It is sometimes associated with bias because a model with high training error is likely too simple to capture the underlying patterns. Minimizing training error alone is not sufficient — it is only one piece of the performance puzzle.

bullets.ppt_text: • Generalization error (variance): model error on new data
bullets.ai_comment: Generalization error measures how well the model performs on data it has never encountered before, which is the true goal of any machine learning system. The teacher defines it as 'your error on a new data,' emphasizing that this is the metric that actually matters in practice. It is associated with variance because a model that overfits the training data will fluctuate wildly on new inputs. A low generalization error means the model has learned the underlying patterns rather than memorizing specific examples.

bullets.ppt_text: • Example: practice a future exam with past exams
bullets.ai_comment: The teacher introduces an intuitive analogy comparing machine learning to student exam preparation: past exams are the training data, and the future exam is the new, unseen data. This analogy makes the abstract concept immediately relatable to students. Just as practicing old exams helps but does not guarantee success on a new exam, training on historical data does not guarantee good performance on future data. This example sets up the more specific contrast between Student A and Student B.

bullets.ppt_text: • Doing well on past exams (training error) doesn't guarantee a  good score on the future exam (generalization error)
bullets.ai_comment: This is the central takeaway of the slide, and the teacher states it directly: 'the performance on the training data does not represent the performance on a new data set.' Achieving a very low or even zero training error can actually be a warning sign, as it may indicate the model has memorized the training data rather than learning generalizable patterns. This concept is the core motivation behind techniques like regularization, cross-validation, and held-out test sets.

bullets.ppt_text: • Student A gets 0 error on past exams by rote learning
bullets.ai_comment: Student A is the analogy for an overfitting model: by memorizing every answer from past exams (training data), they achieve perfect performance on those exams (zero training error). However, this rote learning strategy provides no understanding that can transfer to a new exam (new data), so the generalization error remains high. This is directly parallel to a neural network that memorizes training samples rather than learning the underlying function.

bullets.ppt_text: • Student B  understands the reasons for givenanswers
bullets.ai_comment: Student B represents a well-generalizing model: instead of memorizing, they grasp the underlying principles and reasoning behind each answer. This deeper understanding allows them to handle novel questions on the future exam effectively, resulting in a low generalization error. In machine learning terms, a model like Student B has learned the true underlying data distribution rather than surface-level patterns, which is the ultimate objective of model training.
